Slow-growing and best not heavily harvested.

Known more as animal browse and famine food than as a casual wild edible.

records.scientific_name: Morchella angusticeps and other black morels

mushroom_profile.scientific_name: Morchella angusticeps and other black morels
mushroom_profile.summary: Eastern black morels have vertically oriented pits and ridges that darken with age. They are choice edibles when cooked, but should never be confused with false morels.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Spring, often earlier than yellow morels.
research_notes: Classic eastern black morel concept aligns with Morchella angusticeps.
Ridges typically darken with maturity and the cap meets the stem with a small groove.
Classic eastern black morels are frequently reported around dying elm, ash, old apple, and tulip poplar, but host patterns vary by site and year.
Source coverage count in current app data: 2
Image status in current repo build: needs image

manual_review_reasons: black morel image still needs to be imported
image still missing in repo build

records.culinary_uses: Sautéed, grilled, or added to stocks and soups for depth of flavor

mushroom_profile.summary: Yellow morels have ridges that do not darken black with age. They are highly regarded edibles when cooked thoroughly.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Spring; often later than black morels.
research_notes: North American yellow morel naming is messy; this entry is best treated as a complex.
Cap attaches to the stem without the obvious groove seen in black morels.
Eastern yellow morels are often reported near dying elm, ash, apple, and other hardwoods; they can also show up in mixed urban and riparian settings.
Source coverage count in current app data: 2
Image status in current repo build: needs image

mushroom_profile.summary: A bright orange-yellow shelf polypore with pores instead of gills. Usually found on hardwoods, especially oak, and often best when young and tender.
research_notes: Some people react poorly to chicken of the woods, especially older specimens or some host associations.
Young edge tissue is usually the best culinary material.

mushroom_profile.summary: A dark, funnel- to trumpet-shaped ground mushroom that is easy to miss visually but distinctive once found. The fertile surface is smooth to shallowly wrinkled rather than truly gilled.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Mid to late summer into fall.
research_notes: Upper and lower surfaces are both dark at first; the outer fertile surface lightens somewhat with spore maturity.
Eastern black trumpets are usually associated with mature deciduous woods, especially oak and beech, sometimes with maple mixed in.

Image status in current repo build: has image

mushroom_profile.summary: A classic field Agaricus with pink-then-brown free gills, a ring, and terrestrial growth in grass. It is edible, but confusion with toxic Agaricus and deadly Amanita species is a serious concern.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer into fall in grassy sites.
research_notes: This app entry should be treated as campestris-like rather than a guarantee of true Agaricus campestris.
Beginner users need a warning about white-gilled Amanitas and yellow-staining or phenolic-smelling Agaricus lookalikes.

mushroom_profile.summary: A prized porcini-type bolete with a reticulate stem, white to yellow-olive pore surface, and generally non-blue-staining flesh.
research_notes: North American porcini-like mushrooms are complicated; this is best treated as an edulis-group entry.
Look for a stout stem, fine reticulation, and pores that start whitish and age yellow-olive.

mushroom_profile.summary: Huge terrestrial puffball that is edible only while the interior is pure white and uniform. Once the interior yellows or browns, it is past eating stage.
research_notes: One of the easier edibles to understand once cut open.
Interior color is the make-or-break field clue.
Source coverage count in current app data: 2
Image status in current repo build: needs image

records.culinary_uses: Sautéed, grilled, or added to stocks and soups for depth of flavor

mushroom_profile.summary: A tall, shaggy-scaled inky cap that appears in lawns and disturbed ground. Best only when young and still white before the gills liquefy into black ink.
research_notes: The rapid inky deliquescence is one of its best field characters.
This one is more a freshness race than a long-lasting fridge mushroom.
Source coverage count in current app data: 2
Image status in current repo build: has image

records.culinary_uses: Sautéed, grilled, or added to stocks and soups for depth of flavor

mushroom_profile.summary: Hedgehog mushrooms are ground-growing mycorrhizal mushrooms with soft teeth or spines under the cap instead of gills or pores.
research_notes: A nice beginner character is the toothed underside.
Species boundaries in Hydnum are messier than old field guides imply.
Source coverage count in current app data: 1
Image status in current repo build: has image

records.culinary_uses: Sautéed, grilled, or added to stocks and soups for depth of flavor

mushroom_profile.summary: A slimy-capped Suillus with pores and a ring, strongly associated with pines. Many foragers peel the cap cuticle before cooking.
research_notes: The sticky brown cap and developing purple shades on the ring are strong clues.

Image status in current repo build: has image

mushroom_profile.summary: A small brownish chanterelle relative with yellow stem and ridged underside, often found in mossy northern conifer habitat.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Late summer into fall in cool, damp conifer habitat.
research_notes: Often fruits in moss, including over well decayed moss-covered logs.

research_notes: This entry probably duplicates the meadow-mushroom concept and should eventually be merged or clarified.

mushroom_profile.summary: A toothed wood-growing mushroom with long hanging spines and no cap. Typically found on hardwoods, especially oaks, from wounds or dead wood.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Late summer into fall; can persist into winter in milder weather.
research_notes: If the entry is being used broadly for lion’s mane and its cousins, split Hericium species later.

mushroom_profile.summary: A rosette of flattened or frilly branches growing from roots or the bases of trees. Eastern entries may represent more than one Sparassis species.
research_notes: Sparassis spathulata in eastern North America can be under oaks or pines.
If the app wants precision later, split eastern S. spathulata from conifer-rooting S. americana concepts.

mushroom_profile.summary: A superb edible oyster, but one that is primarily a Mediterranean/Asian wild species and is commonly encountered in North America as a cultivated mushroom rather than a wild Upper Midwest find.
mushroom_profile.ecology: saprobic / weakly parasitic on herbaceous hosts
mushroom_profile.season_note: In practice this is often a cultivated entry rather than a local wild one.
research_notes: This should likely be reviewed for whether it belongs in a wild Upper Michigan app.
Wild P. eryngii is native to the Mediterranean and Asia and is tied to Apiaceae hosts rather than wood like most oyster mushrooms.
This is primarily a cultivated mushroom for North American users; keep the entry flagged until the app decides whether cultivated species belong in scope.

mushroom_profile.summary: A lilac to buff saprobe of leaf litter and woodland edges, often with a faint anise-like odor. Older specimens fade and can be confused with lookalikes.
research_notes: The purple-lilac colors fade quickly, so scent and spore color matter.
Good candidate for a strong lookalike caution note.

mushroom_profile.summary: A larch-associated Suillus group with sticky caps, pores, and usually a ring. This is one of the clearest tree-linked entries in the app.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Late summer into fall under larch/tamarack.
research_notes: North American grevillei-like material often goes by Suillus clintonianus, especially under native larches.
This is a good example of why the host-tree filter matters.

mushroom_profile.summary: A cold-weather wood-growing mushroom with a sticky orange-brown cap and a dark velvety stem base. Correctly identified wild velvet foot is edible, but confusion with deadly Galerina is a major caution point.
research_notes: The white spore print and velvety dark stem help separate it from Galerina marginata, but the confusion risk still deserves bold warning language.
Source coverage count in current app data: 3

records.culinary_uses: True morel; excellent cooked, but smaller and more fragile than black and yellow morels.

mushroom_profile.variant_strategy: listed separately because eastern half-free morels are a recognizable true-morel type in hardwood forests east of the Great Plains
mushroom_profile.summary: A true morel with the cap attached only about halfway down the stem, leaving the lower half hanging free. Excellent edible when thoroughly cooked.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Spring; often between black and yellow morels.
research_notes: The eastern half-free morel is Morchella punctipes; the western counterpart is Morchella populiphila.
Treat this as its own true-morel variant rather than hiding it under generic morels.
Strong confusion warning should still point users away from false morels like Gyromitra.
Source coverage count in current app data: 2
Image status in current repo build: needs image

records.culinary_uses: Choice chanterelles for sautéing, drying lightly, eggs, cream sauces, and mixed wild-mushroom dishes.

mushroom_profile.variant_strategy: listed as a hardwood-associated complex because Michigan yellow chanterelles include multiple closely related hardwood-linked taxa
mushroom_profile.summary: Golden to yellow chanterelles associated with hardwoods, especially oak and beech. Fruity, apricot-like aroma and decurrent false gills are key field marks.
research_notes: Michigan material appears to include more than one hardwood-associated yellow chanterelle type.
Oak- and beech-associated yellows are worth separating from conifer-associated yellow chanterelles in the app.
Do not confuse chanterelle ridges with true blade-like gills.
Source coverage count in current app data: 3
Image status in current repo build: needs image

manual_review_reasons: chanterelle taxonomy is still moving; field-complex treatment is intentional

records.culinary_uses: Choice edible chanterelles with similar kitchen use to hardwood yellows.

mushroom_profile.variant_strategy: listed as a conifer-associated complex because Michigan conifer chanterelles appear distinct from oak/beech associates but taxonomy is still being worked out
mushroom_profile.summary: Yellow chanterelles associated with conifers such as spruce and hemlock. In Michigan they seem smaller and squatter than hardwood-associated yellow chanterelles.
research_notes: Midwest American Mycological Information notes UP and northern Michigan conifer-associated yellows that likely represent different species from oak and beech associates.
Useful to split in the app even when exact species names remain unsettled.
Source coverage count in current app data: 2
Image status in current repo build: needs image

manual_review_reasons: chanterelle taxonomy is still moving; field-complex treatment is intentional

records.culinary_uses: Good edible; smaller than yellow chanterelles but useful in the same dishes.

mushroom_profile.variant_strategy: listed as a recognizable red chanterelle type; exact Michigan taxonomy may include close relatives but the field form is coherent
mushroom_profile.summary: Small flamingo-pink to cinnabar-red chanterelles with false gills, usually in eastern hardwood forests.
research_notes: MSU notes the red chanterelle is native to eastern North America.
Michigan material may include more than one red taxon, but this is still a useful field-facing entry.
Source coverage count in current app data: 3

manual_review_reasons: chanterelle taxonomy is still moving; field-complex treatment is intentional

records.culinary_uses: Choice edible chanterelle with a smoother underside and good texture in sautés and sauces.

mushroom_profile.variant_strategy: listed separately because the smooth underside is a practical field distinction, even though the complex likely needs more study
mushroom_profile.summary: A medium to large chanterelle with a smooth to only shallowly wrinkled underside rather than strongly developed false gills.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Usually midsummer in eastern North America.
research_notes: Cantharellus lateritius is a practical app entry, but MushroomExpert notes the North American smooth chanterelle complex likely needs more study.
Separate this from yellow chanterelles because the underside texture is a major field clue.

manual_review_reasons: chanterelle taxonomy is still moving; field-complex treatment is intentional

records.culinary_uses: Edible russulas valued for their seafood-like odor and respectable texture when correctly identified.

mushroom_profile.variant_strategy: listed as a species complex because multiple North American shrimp-russulas share a distinctive odor and similar field marks
mushroom_profile.summary: A complex of russulas with a characteristic shrimp- or fish-like odor, brownish staining, and a dull yellow spore print.
research_notes: Best treated as a complex rather than a single species in North America.
Some eastern forms are associated with eastern white pine, while others occur with different hosts.
A shrimp-like odor is the best field anchor.
Source coverage count in current app data: 2
Image status in current repo build: needs image

manual_review_reasons: russula group should stay field-usable, not over-precise

records.culinary_uses: Good mild russulas when correctly identified; cook before eating.
records.scientific_name: Russula cyanoxantha group / Russula variata group

mushroom_profile.scientific_name: Russula cyanoxantha group / Russula variata group
mushroom_profile.variant_strategy: listed as a field-facing mild complex because North American lookalikes complicate exact species naming
mushroom_profile.summary: Mild-tasting russulas with unusually flexible, soft gills and often mixed green-purple cap colors.
research_notes: The classic charcoal-burner concept is complicated in North America by lookalikes like Russula variata.
The flexible gills are the key practical field clue.
Source coverage count in current app data: 2

manual_review_reasons: russula group should stay field-usable, not over-precise

records.culinary_uses: Edible but often mediocre unless transformed into lobster mushroom by Hypomyces lactifluorum.

mushroom_profile.variant_strategy: listed separately because it is a widespread, recognizable white russula in eastern forests
mushroom_profile.summary: A sturdy white to pale russula with a short stem in proportion to the cap, often partly hidden under leaves or needles.
mushroom_profile.staining: Brownish bruising on gills/stem often present
research_notes: Often overlooked because fruitbodies can be partly buried in litter.
Also important because Hypomyces lactifluorum can parasitize this group and create lobster mushrooms.
Source coverage count in current app data: 2
Image status in current repo build: needs image

manual_review_reasons: russula group should stay field-usable, not over-precise

records.culinary_uses: Edible with caution; many foragers dry them or cook them thoroughly to improve texture and digestibility.

mushroom_profile.variant_strategy: listed as a prudent group because several Leccinum occur in the region and are better grouped by the scabered stem trait unless species-level work is done
mushroom_profile.summary: Boletes with peppered or scabered stems, often associated with birch, aspen, and other hardwoods. Several are edible but must be cooked very well and some people react poorly to them.
mushroom_profile.staining: Variable: none, gray, pinkish, olive, or bluish depending on species
research_notes: The stem scabers are the main field mark.
This group includes birch bolete types and aspen/orange-cap allies; species-level treatment can come later.
Use caution because Leccinum edibility varies by species and by individual tolerance.
Source coverage count in current app data: 3
Image status in current repo build: needs image

manual_review_reasons: bolete handling should favor field-useful groups unless a tighter ID is justified

records.culinary_uses: Not for eating; bitterness can ruin an entire pan or pot.

mushroom_profile.variant_strategy: listed separately because it is a common porcini lookalike that can ruin meals
mushroom_profile.summary: A porcini lookalike with pinkish pores, a netted stem, and an extremely bitter taste.
research_notes: North American material called Tylopilus felleus may not be identical to the European species, but the field concept is still useful.
Important app entry because it is a classic king-bolete spoiler.

manual_review_reasons: bolete handling should favor field-useful groups unless a tighter ID is justified

records.culinary_uses: Caution group only; do not treat as a kitchen entry.

mushroom_profile.variant_strategy: kept as a caution group because several red-pored or strongly blue-staining boletes are difficult for beginners and should not be collapsed into edible porcini-type boletes
mushroom_profile.summary: A caution bucket for boletes with red pores and/or strong blue staining. Some are edible, some are not recommended, and the group is too risky to flatten into one edible entry.
research_notes: Not a single species; this is a safety-oriented grouping.
Use the app to warn beginners away from treating all boletes as edible.
A future pass should split this into actual regional species where data support exists.

manual_review_reasons: bolete handling should favor field-useful groups unless a tighter ID is justified

records.culinary_uses: Choice oyster for frying, soups, stir-fries, and drying.

mushroom_profile.variant_strategy: listed separately from other oysters because season and host patterns differ meaningfully from other Pleurotus in North America
mushroom_profile.summary: The classic cool-weather oyster mushroom, usually brownish to gray and growing in overlapping clusters on hardwood.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Fall through early spring in cool weather.
research_notes: Wood-growing shelf clusters with decurrent gills remain the anchor field marks.
North American oysters are a complex; this entry is for the cool-weather pearl oyster concept.
Source coverage count in current app data: 2
Image status in current repo build: needs image

records.culinary_uses: Good warm-weather oyster for frying and general cooking.

mushroom_profile.variant_strategy: listed separately because it is a warm-weather oyster distinct from the classic cool-weather pearl oyster
mushroom_profile.summary: A paler, warm-weather oyster on hardwoods, usually fruiting from late spring through summer.
research_notes: MSU notes P. pulmonarius is generally found on hardwoods in the eastern United States.
Useful to split from the cool-weather oyster in the app by season as well as species.
Source coverage count in current app data: 2
Image status in current repo build: needs image

records.culinary_uses: Good edible oyster from Populus wood; cook thoroughly.

mushroom_profile.variant_strategy: listed separately because it is a distinct North American oyster tied to Populus hosts
mushroom_profile.summary: A North American oyster that fruits on quaking aspen, cottonwood, and related Populus wood.
research_notes: MushroomExpert treats P. populinus as a well-established biological and phylogenetic species.
This is exactly the kind of host-specific mushroom your tree filters should be able to surface.
Source coverage count in current app data: 2
Image status in current repo build: needs image
